frameworks.name: utility maximization (Generalized Axiom of Revealed Preference)
frameworks.one_line: Tests whether food choices satisfy necessary and sufficient consistency conditions for an underlying utility

frameworks.name: value-based decision making
frameworks.one_line: Options selected by subjective values the animal assigns to goods

frameworks.name: dopamine-dependent reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: Subjective values are learned via intact dopamine signaling

core_question: Does C. elegans choose between bacterial foods as if maximizing an underlying subjective utility?
key_assumptions: GARP consistency is necessary and sufficient for utility maximization
Human-consumer utility functional forms can describe nematode preferences
Chemosensory neuron responses can index assigned subjective value
manipulations: Offered choices between bacterial foods with distinct growth potentials
Manipulated prior consumption experience to test value learning
Combined microfluidic choice assays with electrophysiology of identified neurons
key_findings: Food choices satisfy necessary and sufficient conditions of GARP for utility maximization
Choices are well-fit by a utility function widely used for human consumers
Learning of subjective values requires intact dopamine signaling
Identified chemosensory neurons respond differentially to foods, amplified by prior consumption
interpretations: C. elegans behaves as if maintaining and maximizing an internal representation of subjective value
Chemosensory neurons may be part of a value-assignment system shaped by experience
